rationales.hedonism: He keeps sacrificing comfort and enjoyment, eating "lunch... at my desk again" and abandoning the balcony because "once I picked up the phone it was over."
rationales.achievement: He structures the day around duties and follow-through, from finalizing the roster to updating projections and reviewing "tomorrow's agenda" at home.
rationales.benevolence: He approved a worker's request for "the entire last week of December off" because "he hasn't taken time in months," prioritizing that person's needs.

rationales.hedonism: He tries to enjoy a quiet tea on the balcony but says he "couldn't let myself have it for more than five minutes" before turning it into another problem.
rationales.achievement: Even at "eleven at night," he measures himself against being productive and feels unable to sit still without justification.

rationales.hedonism: The abandoned pleasure is the whole point here: he finds the tea "cold on the railing" after repeatedly failing to actually enjoy it.
He deliberately savors a small pleasure, buying "knafeh on the way home," eating it "still warm," and not checking his phone at all.
He denies himself rest despite being "genuinely tired," stays for emails that "could have waited," and says guilt is "louder than the tiredness."
rationales.conformity: He stays because of "how that would look" if he left at three while the team stayed until five, showing strong pressure from role expectations.
rationales.benevolence: He frames his own rest as different because "if they rest, I'm the one who covered," centering responsibility for protecting his team.

rationales.hedonism: A gathering with "good food and music" becomes joyless because he can't stop thinking about work and leaves early.
rationales.achievement: A looming "procurement deadline on Monday" dominates his attention so completely that it overrides the event he had "looked forward to all week."

rationales.self_direction: The decisive tension is that the mural brief feels like "their story, not mine," and the entry centers wanting to author work on their own terms.
rationales.security: They say they "should probably say yes because I need the money" but feel a tightness in their chest about taking the safer paid option.
rationales.universalism: The family from Syria and the girl's drawings reshape the whole day, and the artist keeps returning to whether the work serves something larger than branding.

rationales.self_direction: They turn down the commission, feel "RELIEVED," and commit to making something real rather than accepting a brief that does not fit their own direction.
rationales.stimulation: The artist is pulled toward a new medium that could "move" and says static murals were "starting to feel like cages," signaling active pursuit of novelty and change.
rationales.achievement: They reject the recognizable paid project and say they'd "rather be broke and making something real than comfortable and decorating," stepping away from conventional success markers.
rationales.security: Choosing "no money in it probably" over the safer commission openly sacrifices stability for conviction.
rationales.conformity: They refuse the expected commercial path and reject simply delivering what clients or the art world would find easy to accept.
rationales.universalism: The proposed next project centers the refugee kids so it can tell "THEIR story instead of a brand's story," driven by concern beyond the self.

rationales.self_direction: He chooses the "very safe" hotel job while saying the honest series is work he "can't show to anyone," suppressing his own artistic path.
rationales.conformity: He explicitly orients around anticipated family reactions like his uncle asking why the work is "so depressing," and defaults to the option others will accept.

rationales.self_direction: He hides a real relationship and gives only "the correct answers," showing he is not acting on what he actually wants to say or disclose.
rationales.conformity: The whole lunch is managed through deflection to avoid upsetting family and community expectations, and he describes himself as efficient at predicting which battles not to start.

rationales.self_direction: He admits the hidden series is "the best work I've ever done" yet still says no to showing it, choosing avoidance over his own artistic voice.
rationales.stimulation: He keeps making work he calls "technically proficient and completely forgettable" instead of pursuing the more challenging, boundary-pushing show.
rationales.conformity: He names the real obstacle as disappointing people and drawing attention he is "not ready for," so he sticks with what keeps things socially smooth.
He describes the engagement party script almost mechanically: "I'll wear the right shirt, say the right things, congratulate the right people," emphasizing compliance with expectations.
He "smiled through all of it" at the party and feels relief when his aunt calls him "a good boy," showing how strongly he is organized around approval and proper behavior.

rationales.self_direction: He admits he could make time for the show but will not because he does not want to reveal work that "actually says something," holding back his own expression.
rationales.conformity: He explicitly chooses to "keep things smooth" and preserve being "predictable" in the eyes of family rather than disrupt that social image.

rationales.self_direction: He nearly posts the work he knows is good, then imagines family reactions and "covered everything back up," retreating from his own artistic choice.
rationales.conformity: What stops him is fear of losing "the approval, the ease, the feeling of fitting in," which he treats as foundational.

rationales.self_direction: His authentic imagery emerges in the hotel piece, but he immediately "painted over them before anyone could see," undoing his own impulse.
rationales.conformity: Even after his sister shows interest, he still tells her the hidden series is "nothing, just experiments," keeping the family-facing version of himself intact.

rationales.self_direction: The praise hurts because the finished piece was "exactly what he envisioned," meaning the artist delivered someone else's vision rather than his own.
rationales.conformity: The commission succeeds precisely by matching the client's expectations, and his depressed reaction highlights how much he has been accommodating external demands.

rationales.self_direction: He finally shows his sister the hidden pieces and admits he may be "getting tired of hiding the work," taking a concrete step toward owning what he actually wants to make.
rationales.conformity: Leaving the work uncovered and noticing that "the tarp felt heavier putting it back on" signals movement away from the old habit of concealment for others' comfort.
